title: PDF Page 1
content:
Administrative Issues 
(The originator of the Issue has been removed) 
 
Date  Issue Raised CPR Help Desk Response 
June 27, 2003 Where can one access copies of the Staff Instruction 
mentioned in Policy Letter 500-002?  
Formal copies have been mailed out, for the 
time being copies are available through 
RDIMS (only available to TC). 
July 03, 2003 
and 
February 20, 2004 
How do we respond to a request for an addition of a 
new registration (or serial number) to a previously 
issued LSTC. 
No assessment is required if the same make 
and model is being added to an existing 
certificate. 
August 10, 2004 
 
Is there a requirement to identify in the STC/LSTC 
document the classification of the design change 
(significant or not significant) 
There is no requirement to identify in the 
STC/LSTC document itself the 
classification of the design change.  The 
classification of the design change must be 
captured and recorded in the decision 
record or Issue paper. 
January 21, 2005 
 
FAA HQ Memo cited SFAR 41 as a terminated 
standard, and can no longer be used for issuing 
amended type certificates or supplemental type 
certificates.  Instead, FAR 23 at Amendment 34 is the 
earliest amendment level when performing 
modifications on areas listed in SFAR 41. 
 
Should TCCA follow this policy? 
  
TCCA will continue to allow use of SFAR 
41 as a certification basis only for not 
significant change. 
 
The FAA is aware of this Memo that was 
issued pre-CPR and is currently evaluating 
the Memo. 
August 26, 2005 
 
An applicant submitted an STC application before 
CPR came into effect, and we agreed to comply with 
the existing certification basis.  We are close to 
issuing a STC for this project and would like to know 
if the FAA will require special requirements for 
accepting STCs issued in 2005. 
 
The date of application is what determines 
whether CPR applies or not, not the date of 
issue of the STC.  In this case, the 
application was received prior to CPR 
taking into effect; therefore, there is no 
obligation to comply with the latest 
standards.

title: PDF Page 2
content:
Technical Issues 
(The originator of the Issue has been removed) 
 
Date  Issue Raised CPR Help Desk Response 
September 15, 2003 An applicant is proposing a significant change 
involving retrofit of a turbine engine and a higher 
capacity floats on a DHC-3.  Applicant wants 
guidance on how to pursue the CPR exception for 
“not practical” 
There must be substantiation against each 
applicable latest airworthiness standards 
that can convince TCCA to allow such 
reversion to an earlier amendment. 
October 10, 2003 I need some guidance on what an applicant is obliged 
to consider concerning “affected areas” and the need 
to re-certify these areas 
 
Areas that are affected, and are part of a 
previous design change approval, need to 
be re-assessed, and if required, re-certified 
under the new design change.   
October 27, 2003 
 
If a small airplane (Piper PA-12) was progressively 
retrofitted with engines of higher power over a period 
of years (say from 115HP to 160 HP, and now 180 
HP) is the modification considered significant 
(considering that the upgrade is to restore 
performance when aircraft is retrofitted with floats 
from another STC)? 
TCCA and the applicant argued that such 
design change could be considered as not 
significant.  The CPR Help Desk 
concurred. 
January 26, 2004 An applicant wants to apply for a new STC for a 
cargo door installation on a DHC-7, based on a 
previously approved STC.  The cargo door itself is 
approved under the aircraft’s type certificate data 
sheet.  Should the classification of the new STC take 
into account the history of previously approved data 
for this door installation? 
   
Since it is a new STC application, CPR will 
apply and this change will be classified as 
significant.  However, the previously 
approved data could be used to invoke the 
exceptions clause of CPR for arguing 
compliance with other than the latest 
standards, citing no material contribution to 
safety or impractical, based on service 
experience. 
January 28, 2004 A CASA 212 is changing it’s propellers from plastic 
to metal, is this change deemed “not significant”?  
At an aircraft level, the change in propeller 
material is considered not significant. 
June 18, 2004 A manufacturer submitted application for an 
amendment to a type certificate data sheet for the 
addition of a new helicopter model.  The applicant 
claims that the change is significant.   
 
CPR Help Desk expressed an opinion that 
the quantity and extent of the design 
changes being proposed is likely that the 
design change would be classified as a 
substantial change.  Another opinion was 
expressed supporting the applicant’s 
assessment of a significant change. 
 
(The two opinions were given to TCCA 
management and the applicant for a 
discussion.  Eventually, the applicant re-
submit their application for a new type 
certificate) 
August 25, 2005 We are modifying an HS-748 to create a Class “E” 
cargo compartment.  The aircraft already has a 
crew/freight door as part of its type certificate, and 
we are adding structural provisions for barrier nets, 
intercostals to one area of the floor, adding cargo 
liners, lights and smoke detection system.  We 
believe our modification is not significant, please 
confirm our decision. 
 
The classification of the proposed design 
change for this proposal is "significant", by 
default under CAR 521.  But having 
recognized the merit of the submission by 
the applicant, you may consider accepting 
the change to be "not significant" provided 
you qualify such acceptance under the one 
of the three criteria defined in the 
regulations
